# Page 1^page=1
Autonomous Operation Enabled by Asset Administration Shell-Based Fault Handling in Data Spaces 1st Pascal R¨ubel Technologie-Initiative SmartFactory KL Kaiserslautern, Germany https://orcid.org/0000-0003-4750-7012 2nd Simon Jungbluth Technologie-Initiative SmartFactory KL Kaiserslautern, Germany https://orcid.org/0009-0005-1225-1784 3 rd Marcel Ely Gomes Digital Solutions Research TRUMPF Ditzingen, Germany https://orcid.org/0009-0004-0933-1911 4 th Tom K¨orner Digital Solutions Research TRUMPF Ditzingen, Germany https://orcid.org/0009-0006-7878-7669 5th Benjamin Blumhofer Technologie-Initiative SmartFactory KL Kaiserslautern, Germany https://orcid.org/0000-0002-0983-1690 6th Martin Ruskowski Technologie-Initiative SmartFactory KL Kaiserslautern, Germany https://orcid.org/0000-0002-6534-9057 Abstract—Increasing manufacturing heterogeneity, shorter time-to-market, and a shortage of skilled labor demand more ﬂexible and resilient production systems. However, efﬁcient fault handling is often hindered by a lack of interoperable models and concerns over data sovereignty. This paper introduces a framework that enables assisted and autonomous operations through a combination of standardized digital twins and secure data space mechanisms (MX-Port). By utilizing Asset Admin- istration Shells (AAS), OPC UA, and the Eclipse Dataspace Connector, the MX-Port facilitates a remote operator panel for cross-company monitoring and control. The approach is validated through two demonstrators: the AAS-based MX-Port Hercules at SmartFactory-KL and the OPC UA-based MX-Port Orion at Trumpf. These use cases demonstrate how standardized semantic models and decentralized access control provide a scalable solution for interoperable data provisioning and resilient shop ﬂoor operations in complex, sovereign industrial environments. Index Terms—Manufacturing-X, Data Space, Skill-Based Pro- duction, Fault Recovery, Remote Operation I. INTRODUCTION Smaller product batch sizes and shorter time to market require higher ﬂexibility in manufacturing systems. At the time, manufacturing environments are highly heterogeneous, increasing the complexity for technological solutions [1], [2]. The shortage of skilled human workers is stressing the production situation additionally [3]. The handling of faults is difﬁcult since they rarely occur, and there are no semantic and interoperable information models available due to high heterogeneity. The tooling situation is difﬁcult because of data sovereignty, access control, and maintaining production quality [4], [5]. The Manufacturing-X Port (MX-Port) [5] based on standardized and semantic models like Asset Administration Shell (AAS) and Open Platform Communications Uniﬁed This work was supported by the German Federal Ministry of Economic Af- fairs and Energy by the project ”Aufbau eines Datenraums f¨ur die ausr¨ustende Industrie – die Ausr¨uster von Fabriken weltweit” (Factory-X) under grant 13MX001ZU. Architecture (OPC UA) Companion Speciﬁcations as well as decentralized access and usage control can be a solution for interoperable data provisioning via data spaces. In this paper, resilient and efﬁcient production operations are developed that enable assisted, semi-autonomous, and autonomous operations using a combination of standardized digital twins, secure data space mechanisms, and analytic services. Accordingly, an interoperable Remote Operator Panel (ROP) is introduced that bundles cross-company data exchange in the backend and delivers a frontend for a human worker monitoring and controlling machines remotely. Therefore, AAS Submodels (SM) for fault handling and asset interfaces description, as well as OPC UA Companion Speciﬁcations (e.g., for machinery [6] and machine tools [7]) for machine data submission are used for semantic information modeling and Application Programming Interfaces (API). Additionally, the Factory-X Eclipse Dataspace Connector (F-X EDC) is introduced for discovery as well as access and usage control to enable the communication between the ROP and the shop ﬂoor. A proof of concept is provided by a demonstrator at SmartFactory-KL model factory using MX-Port Hercules leveraging AAS and F-X EDC and a demonstrator at Trumpf using MX-Port Orion building up on OPC UA and F-X EDC. The paper is structured as follows: Section II gives a brief overview of the state of the art. In Section III, the elaborated architecture and the process are described. To demonstrate the work, Section IV is showing two demonstrators for autonomous, semi-autonomous, and assisted fault handling. Finally, a discussion and an outlook are given in Section V. II. STATE OF THE ART A. Asset Administration Shell Standardized and semantic digital representations of assets are a key factor for realizing interoperability for industrial assets. The AAS is introduced as a reference model for

# Page 2^page=2
the representation of Industrie 4.0 assets in a standardized manner, thus being formally represented in the international speciﬁcation IEC63278-1 [8]. Depending on the communication requirements of the use case, it can be chosen between three different types of AAS [9], [10]. • Type 1: The AAS is represented as a ﬁle that stores information about the asset during its lifecycle, e.g., AASX, JSON or XML. This type is referred to as passive • Type 2: Online access to asset information is enabled by an API that responds to external requests. Therefore, this type is called reactive AAS. • Type 3: The proactive AAS comes with its own decision- making and the ability to initiate action. Type 3 AAS are capable of interacting and collaborating with other AAS. The AAS has a modular structure consisting of so called SMs, whereas an SM deﬁnes a speciﬁc aspect of the AAS. It contains a structured collection of Submodel Elements (SME) (such as properties, ﬁles, or operations) that describe the asset from a particular perspective (e.g., its conﬁguration, status, or capabilities). SMs ensure interoperability by following stan- dardized templates, allowing different systems to understand the asset’s data without manual integration [10]. The SMs can be standardized with the Industrial Digital Twin Association (IDTA) to reach a high level of interoperability. Additionally to the standardization of submodels, the IDTA elaborated and distributed speciﬁcations for different parts of the AAS. By leveraging its modular SM architecture and technology- independent design, the AAS serves as a vital cornerstone for industrial digital twins, harmonizing interoperable data ex- change across diverse systems and organizational boundaries. Through the standardization of interfaces between physical assets and digital ecosystems, it enables scalable automation solutions and consistent data governance. Consequently, it establishes the essential framework for complex use cases such as plug-and-produce integration, predictive maintenance, and cross-enterprise data sharing. B. OPC UA Interoperability between industrial systems requires a shared communication framework. Alongside the AAS, OPC UA is a widely adopted, vendor-neutral architecture that pro- vides both secure communication and semantic information modeling that has established itself as the de-facto standard for Industrie 4.0 communication [11]. OPC UA addresses interoperability at multiple levels: its transport layer deﬁnes communication patterns, including client-server and publish- subscribe [12] mechanisms; its information model provides a standardized structural grammar; and its Companion Spec- iﬁcations deﬁne domain-speciﬁc vocabularies. Notably, OPC UA for Machinery [6] enables cross-industry interoperability through a standardized base vocabulary for mechanical and plant engineering. It encompasses machine and component identiﬁcation, as well as the machinery state, which reﬂects the operational condition of a machine and is of particular relevance during the production phase. Furthermore, it sup- ports the modeling of process values, including metadata, alarms triggered by deviations from deﬁned parameters, and job management information such as job status, results, and intermediate results. Building upon this foundation, the Com- panion Speciﬁcation OPC UA for Machine Tools [7] extends OPC UA for Machinery with a focus on machine tool-speciﬁc C. Skill-Based Production Skill-based engineering tends to increase ﬂexibility and changeability regarding new products to be manufactured in dynamic industrial automation systems. Intentionally, Malakuti et al. [13] describes a skill in four dimensions in terms of their independence from products, resources, and processes, as well as their structure concerning atomic and composed skills. The so-called Capability Skill Service (CSS) Model [14], [15] en- hances this approach by consolidating the terminology around capabilities, skills, and services. The factory’s functionality is described in an abstract manner, with capabilities as an implementation-independent function in industrial production, skills as the executable implementation of an encapsulated function, and services describing commercial aspects and means of provision. Often skills are implemented with OPC UA, as shown, e.g., in [16] and [17]. Therefore, skills are comparable with Remote Procedure Calls (RPC) where an OPC UA client can control and monitor the process. A comprehensive approach to how skills can be integrated into existing OPC UA information models is provided by [18]. Here, skills are modeled as so-called AddIns to enhance OPC UA for Machinery. D. Data Spaces Cross-domain data spaces constitute a fundamental prereq- uisite for data exchange in multi-enterprise environments, as they provide mechanisms to establish trust, ensure interop- erability, and preserve data sovereignty across organizational boundaries. These capabilities form the foundation for efﬁ- cient and resilient production operations. The International Data Spaces Association (IDSA) speciﬁes the IDS Reference Architecture [19], which introduces a layered model that provides a comprehensive blueprint for industrial data spaces, enabling the realization of secure and trusted environments for assisted, semi-autonomous, and autonomous industrial operations. From a technical-processual perspective, the IDS Reference Architecture deﬁnes the component interaction in the system layer as shown in Figure 1. The Eclipse Dataspace Connector (EDC) is a technical man- ifestation of the speciﬁcation as it implements the IDSA data space protocol. Over time, various derivatives emerged, such as Tractus-X EDC or F-X EDC, providing domain-speciﬁc features. These connectors deﬁne how data is shared, but not what kind of data is exchanged. As different stakeholders require different communication technologies, the MX-Port [5] constitutes the complete technical data exchange stack. MX-Port consists of ﬁve layers responsible for fetching data

# Page 3^page=3
Fig. 1. IDS dataspace components interaction (adapter), processing data semantically (converter), exposing data in a uniform way (gate), restricting access and usage (access and usage control), and ﬁnding data (discovery). While F-X EDC is responsible for discovery as well as access and usage control layer, OPC UA and AAS are used for the gate and converter layer. Different MX-Port conﬁgurations are named accordingly. For us, MX-Port Hercules (F-X EDC and AAS) and Orion (F-X EDC and AAS) are relevant. Finally, Factory-X provides various Network and Use Case Architecture Decision Records (ADR) [20] that deﬁne how to implement a corresponding transaction. E. Fault Handling As manufacturing systems evolve toward modular, skill- based architectures, the operational demands placed on fault management have grown considerably. The structural com- plexity inherent to Cyber-Physical Production System (CPPS), combined with the runtime ﬂexibility they are expected to provide, creates conditions under which failures occur more frequently and manifest in forms that are difﬁcult to foresee during system design [21]. Conventional control software addresses this by incorporating predeﬁned recovery routines for a limited set of known failure cases. However, this approach is fundamentally constrained to failure scenarios, that were explicitly anticipated at engineering time, leaving a potentially large class of runtime faults unhandled. Closing this gap requires fault handling mechanisms that are capable of generating recovery responses dynamically, without relying on exhaustive prior enumeration of all possible fault conditions [21]. From a process perspective, fault handling progresses through a sequence of increasingly speciﬁc diagnostic steps before any corrective action is taken. The ﬁrst step, detecting whether a fault is present at all, is non-trivial in ﬂexible production contexts, where the diversity of executed skills and operational states limits the applicability of data-intensive phenomenological detection methods due to insufﬁcient fault observations [22]. Once a fault has been signaled, its origin must be narrowed down to a speciﬁc subsystem or process variable, enabling root cause attribution rather than mere symptom observation [21], [23]. The subsequent characteriza- tion step, establishing the fault’s nature, extent, and temporal proﬁle, then provides the structured basis upon which recovery decisions can be made [21]. For skill-based production, a fault diagnosis model was developed in [24]. The downstream recovery phase translates this diagnostic output into targeted corrective actions. A process pipeline and required interop- erable information models for the diagnosis and correction determination and execution are elaborated in [25]. Given the unpredictability of fault scenarios in dynamically reconﬁgured production environments, statically pre-programmed recovery logic is insufﬁcient. AI-based planning approaches, which synthesize recovery action sequences automatically from a description of the current system state and available capabili- ties, represent a principled path toward runtime-adaptive fault resolution that scales with system complexity [26]. III. AUTONOMOUS OPERATION & FAULT HANDLING IN DATA SPACES To operationalize the concepts of Section II within a cross- company setting, the proposed architecture adopts and extends the fault diagnosis and correction pattern speciﬁed in Use Case ADR 105: “Fault Diagnosis and Correction” [27]. In this record, fault handling is formalized as an event-driven, data-space-enabled pipeline. A. Roles The fault handling pipeline consists of three primary roles:Fault Data Provider — typically the Machine Owner, who operates the physical asset and exposes fault-relevant • Fault Resolution Expert — usually the Machine Builder or a Component Supplier, who maintains in-depth tech- nical knowledge and fault semantics. • Solution Provider — the Machine (Remote) Operator, who uses the available data and expert knowledge to derive and provide corrections. The interaction among these roles is mediated by the data space and governed by standardized interface and data-model B. Standardized Interfaces and Interoperability To ensure secure and interoperable interaction, each role exposes its data and services according to a set of ADRs from the Factory-X ecosystem for MX-Port Hercules [28]. In the context of the technical architecture depicted in Figure 2, these ADRs deﬁne how participants authenticate against the data space, discover each other’s services, and exchange events and data in a standardized way. For instance, the AAS proﬁle (ADR 008) and AAS-REST-DSP (ADR 009) specify how assets and their submodels are represented and exchanged

# Page 4^page=4
within the data space. A Digital Twin Registry (DTR) is used to discover AAS related data with F-X EDC. ADR 011 and ADR 012 describe how AAS events are emitted and transported using MQTT-over-DSP. This enables the MX-Port components (Hercules and Orion) to seamlessly integrate into the data space, encapsulating the complexity of the underlying The key difference in the application of the different MX- Port conﬁgurations results from the complexity of fault con- textualization. With MX-Port Orion, plain machine data is published, whereby the Solution Provider must determine the relevant data, analyze it, and decide whether to intervene. With MX-Port Hercules, the OPC UA data is preprocessed on the Fault Data Provider’s side. Consequently, the data source remains the same for both conﬁgurations. Preprocessing ensures that only relevant data is consolidated into an SM element. This deﬁnes the context, and the Fault Data Provider decides whether a Solution Provider should intervene. There- fore, the Fault Data Provider is responsible for how the data is provided. The Solution Provider must adhere to the Fault Data Provider’s data format. To have a common entry point AAS with MX-Port Hercules is used initially. In this context, AAS represents the self-description of the machine to discover available asset interface descriptions. The Asset Interfaces De- scription SM semantically describes the associated endpoints and refers it to the related data space asset. Additional data sources of the machine, like streaming data or MQTT brokers with expected data formats, are explorable. C. Submodel-Based Fault Representation The fault solution pipeline relies on a set of AAS SMs that capture the semantics of faulty situations, fault descriptions, corrective actions, and similarity relations between observed and known faults [27]. The core SMs are: • Situation Log — captures the context of a faulty situation by recording the set of symptoms that occurred within a deﬁned temporal window around the time of detection. This submodel can be interpreted as a “symptom dash- cam”, aggregating relevant telemetry, events, and state • Fault Descriptions — provides detailed descriptions of faults that have led to faulty situations in the past. It con- tains contextual information and references to associated symptoms, without duplicating data, as well as links to appropriate corrective actions. • Similarity Analysis — records how a newly observed situation relates to previously known fault descriptions, including similarity metrics and assignments of situations to fault classes. • Fault Correction Set — groups recommended corrective actions (fault corrections) for speciﬁc faults, including both automated strategies and operator-initiated interven- These submodels are complemented by optional AAS tem- plates (e.g., Asset Interfaces Description, Time Series Data, Handover Documentation, Technical Data, Hierarchical Struc- tures, Capability Description) that provide additional context to the Solution Provider. For example, time-series submodels can supply high-frequency sensor data, while capability de- scriptions support the selection of feasible corrective actions given the machine’s available skills. D. Event-Driven Situation Lifecycle The execution of the fault solution pipeline is triggered by the detection of a faulty situation at the Fault Data Provider. In the autonomous operations architecture, such a detection may originate from the shop ﬂoor (e.g., PLC or edge analytics) or from supervisory applications and is transferred to the data space via the MX-Port. The Situation SME is created as an instance in the Situation Log SM and announced using an AAS event. The end-to-end steps of the pipeline are as follows (cf. [27]): 1) The Fault Data Provider detects a faulty situation within a system or machine and creates a new Situation SME instance in the Situation Log SM. 2) An event of a created submodel element is emitted into the data space containing the Situation SME. The Solution Provider receives this event via MQTT-over- DSP and becomes aware of the new situation. 3) The Solution Provider investigates the Situation SME by retrieving additional SMs (e.g., Time Series Data, Handover Documentation, Technical Data) from the Fault Data Provider and requests Fault Descriptions SMs from one or more Fault Resolution Experts. 4) Using the Situation SME, the retrieved Fault Descrip- tions SMs, and optional context data (including his- torical situations and solutions), the Solution Provider diagnoses the faulty situation. In this step, the occurred Situation SME is assigned to one or more Fault Descrip- tions SMs and this assignment, including quantitative similarity information, is recorded in the Similarity Analysis SM. 5) Based on the linked Fault Descriptions SMs, the Solution Provider obtains the associated Fault Correction Set SM from the Fault Resolution Expert or from its own knowledge base. The Fault Correction Set SM comprises one or more fault corrections, each describing a concrete corrective action or workﬂow. 6) Finally, the Solution Provider provides the selected fault corrections to the Fault Data Provider. Depending on the level of autonomy and the applicable safety and governance constraints, the corrective action is either executed automatically (e.g., via a skills-based interface at the machine) or recommended to a human operator, with all actions and outcomes documented for subse- quent learning. In practice, the Solution Provider may already hold Fault Descriptions SMs and Fault Correction Sets SMs for fre- quently occurring situations, thereby reducing latency and dependence on external experts.

# Page 5^page=5
IV. DEMONSTRATIVE IMPLEMENTATION: APPLICATION OF THE FINDINGS WITHIN TWO USE CASE SCENARIOS This part of the paper is divided into the Subsections IV-A and IV-B, each describing a demonstrator with a different MX- Port conﬁguration. The demonstrators have different focuses. In use case 1, data is provided by MX-Port Orion based on OPC UA and F-X EDC. Raw sensor data is exchanged based on OPC UA for Machinery with the data consumer responsible for making the decision on when to interfere and provide recovery strategies. In this case, the consumer actively triggers the provider. The DTR and the interface descriptions to the data sources are provided by Asset Interfaces Description SM in the AAS. The scenario shows mainly the data usage for an assisted remote service operator. In use case 2, data and interface descriptions are provided via AAS and MX-Port Hercules. Data is contextualized on the provider side and only shared if a faulty situation is notiﬁed in a speciﬁed SM. In this case, the provider actively triggers the consumer to solve an occurring fault. Since the focus is more about autonomous and semi-autonomous operation, there are more AAS SMs implemented to provide necessary information for the autonomous fault diagnosis and correction. The SMs are elaborated in subsection IV-B. Machine data and control are encapsulated in machine skills and made accessible via A. Data-Assisted Remote Service Operation via Data Space The demonstrator for this use case is a highly automated laser processing cell manufactured by a leading German ma- chine tool builder. The machine represents a contemporary, fully enclosed production cell, integrating a laser source, a multi-axis NC-controlled motion system, and an automated workpiece handling unit within a single enclosed cabin. Its control architecture comprises three interdependent layers: a touch-based HMI providing the operator interface, a PLC governing safety interlocks and peripheral device coordination, and an NC controller executing the part programs and motion trajectories. The cell is designed for high-volume, largely unattended operation with minimal manual intervention during productive runs. In the context of this work, the cell serves as the data source for real-world fault events encountered during production, including nozzle collisions, interrupted cutting processes, axis positioning errors, and laser source faults. A set of cameras is permanently installed at ﬁxed positions within the machine interior, covering the work envelope from multiple perspectives; these provide the visual modality that complements the electrical and control-layer signals during fault analysis. An overview of the demonstrator is shown in Figure 2. The analysis and resolution of machine tool downtime in this production environment requires the simul- taneous evaluation of heterogeneous data streams that span organizational boundaries, including real-time process signals, structured machine descriptions, and visual inspection data. A data space-based architecture addresses this requirement by enabling controlled, ﬂexible, sovereign data exchange be- tween manufacturers, machine operators, and external ser- Fig. 2. Recommendation pipeline via data space vice providers without requiring centralized data storage or unrestricted data access. Within such an architecture, fault handling is elevated from a locally executed, design-time- bounded procedure to a cross-organizational, runtime-adaptive process that draws on distributed knowledge and analytical At the production system level, signals are generated from the machine’s HMI, PLC, and NC controller in accordance with the OPC UA for Machinery speciﬁcation (OPC 40001-1 [6] and OPC 40501-1 [7]). These signals are exposed by a factory-internal OPC UA server and forwarded via an edge device — a network-connected gateway within the factory infrastructure — to a cloud-hosted MQTT broker instance, making them continuously available to authorized consumers in the data space. When the MachineryItemState of the ma- chine transitions to OutOfService [6], signaling an unscheduled standstill, a video recording spanning 30 seconds prior to and 10 seconds following the event is captured simultaneously from the permanently installed interior cameras. The resulting data — OPC UA signals and event videos — are subsequently made available through the data space. In parallel, an AAS for the machine provides not only technical identiﬁcation and component data but also an Asset Interface Description SM, through which both live camera streams and recorded event videos can be retrieved. The event videos themselves are hosted centrally in a cloud operated by the respective video provider. These data streams are made available to authorized service consumers via F-X EDC instances operating on both the provider and consumer side, with access and usage control policies enforced at the data space layer to govern what data may be shared under which conditions. Contract negotiation and token-based authorization are handled transparently by the connector infrastructure prior to any data transfer.

# Page 6^page=6
On the consumer side, Solution Providers — personnel tasked remotely with resolving machine standstills — are supported by a dedicated review tool through which previously recorded fault events can be annotated with expert knowl- edge, including identiﬁed causes, diagnostic assessments, and applied corrective measures. This accumulated expert knowl- edge, combined with the temporally synchronized OPC UA signals captured around each standstill event, serves as the foundation for a knowledge graph. The graph captures struc- tured relationships between fault identiﬁers, their associated causes, applied ﬁxes, diagnostic procedures, triggered events, relevant camera perspectives, operator messages, and fault categories, thereby enabling similarity-based retrieval of fault patterns from historical cases. The resolution recommendation is generated by a multi-agent system organized in a hier- archical architecture. A central orchestration agent governs the diagnostic workﬂow, fuses the incoming multi-modal data streams with historical analogies retrieved from the knowledge graph, and coordinates the specialized agents responsible for each information modality. It delegates knowledge retrieval to a dedicated knowledge agent, which queries the knowledge graph for causes, ﬁxes, and diagnostic information associated with the identiﬁed fault. Both the orchestration agent and the knowledge agent are realized using large language models, with their behavior shaped through systematic prompt tun- ing to align responses with the domain-speciﬁc requirements of industrial fault diagnosis. Visual analysis is coordinated through a central camera agent, which selects appropriate camera perspectives for the given fault context — reﬂecting the observation that certain faults correlate with preferred viewing angles — and delegates the actual video evaluation to special- ized per-camera agents. These camera agents employ a video language model, speciﬁcally Qwen2.5-VL [29], enabling the direct interpretation of the recorded event video sequences in the context of the fault under investigation. Once all modalities have been evaluated, the orchestration agent consolidates the results and forwards them to a dedicated recommender agent, which synthesizes the available evidence into a ranked set of resolution strategies and presents these as prioritized action recommendations to the Solution Provider. As conﬁdence in the pipeline increases, the same action sequences can be passed directly to an AI planner for autonomous execution, closing the fault handling loop from initial detection through to veriﬁed recovery without mandatory human intervention. B. AAS-Enabled Autonomous and Semi-Autonomous Machine Operation This use case is demonstrated at the SmartFactoryKL model factory. The participating resources (Cyber-Physical Produc- tion Modules (CPPM)) are self-described using the AAS. The functionalities of these resources are encapsulated into, which are formally speciﬁed and made accessible via OPC UA. This enables vendor-independent monitoring and control of the CPPM behavior. See [18] for the description of the skill model setup. The integration of the self-description into a fully digital twin driven ecosystem is described in the SmartFactory KL reference architecture [30]. An overview of the machine setup is visualized in Figure 3. During the production process, fault events are ﬁrst detected and subsequently diagnosed. For the detection of the fault events, deﬁned features for each skill are monitored and compared to their expected nominal behavior. Once the limit of deviation is reached, the skill is halted and an alarm event is ﬁred (see the red colored part of Figure 3). This is implemented in the OPC UA skill implementation. The alarm triggers a fault diagnosis pipeline that includes the Situation SME generation that is a collection of all relevant data and context information that are present at the moment of the occurrence of faulty event. The Situation is represented as an SME and is temporarily added to the SituationLog SM of the CPPM (see Figure 4). Once the Situation SME is added, an AAS event is triggered that a Solution Provider can listen to. Also, the internal part fault diagnosis pipeline is started, and the ROP is informed via AAS eventing over the MX-Port that an anomalous situation occurred (see the orange part of Figure 3). This diagnostic procedure involves classifying the issue based on previously identiﬁed, identical, or similar faults, followed by the recommendation of potential autonomous remedies. A particular emphasis is placed on the interoperable speciﬁcation of the resources, necessary information, and data exchange. To ensure this interoperable compatibility, SMs for the description of faults, their symptoms, and for the correction of faults are utilized throughout the system as part of the IT data layer Fig. 3. Autonomous fault handling architecture and pipeline

# Page 7^page=7
Fig. 4. Example of Situation Submodel Element (right) creation based on OPC UA (left) shown in Figure 3. An example for the SymptomDescription, FaultDescription and FaultCorrection SMs is shown in Figure 5. The actual diagnosis is executed using a similarity anal- ysis that compares the Situation SME with the modeled FaultDescription SM. As a result, another SM is generated, representing the results in the form of similarities to existing faults. If the similarity is not high enough, human intervention by the ROP is required. If a plausible fault is identiﬁed, the related fault correction possibilities are elaborated. They are separated into three categories: autonomous, semi-autonomous and manual correction. In the autonomous case, a skill sequence is referenced in the FaultCorrection SM that is capable of solving the problem and that can be executed automatically. Therefore, the ROP derives the situation-dependent skill parameters. Consequently, the modeled AAS operation in the FaultCorrection SM is called with the situation and the skill as an input to start the skill (see the green part in IT/OT layer in Figure 3). The semi-autonomous case requires remote human interven- tion; this occurs when the similarity analysis lacks the required conﬁdence threshold to proceed with full autonomy. Therefore, the ROP can take over the control of the CPPM and start the necessary skills remotely via MX-Port. Required information, e.g., the SituationLog SM can be accessed by the AAS via MX-Port as well (see the green part at the top of Figure 3). In the third case, the problem can neither be solved au- tonomously nor remotely. Then manual human intervention on the shop ﬂoor is required. Additionally, there can be safety issues or remote execution uncertainties due to which the remote execution should not be executed. The parameters are passed to the shop ﬂoor operator, that starts the execution. After the fault is handled, the data from the Situation SME is transferred to the FaultDescription and SymptomDescription SM and the temporarily stored Situation SME is deleted from the CPPM AAS. Fig. 5. Example fault-related submodels and their relations V. DISCUSSION & OUTLOOK This paper presents an AAS-based autonomous, semi- autonomous and assisted operation scenario using skill-based and data space approaches. The contribution describes the required architecture to enable the autonomous operation scenario using the MX-Port concept in accordance with IDS reference architecture. The AAS-based technical set up is mod- eled to establish an interoperable digital twin that describes the connection to the ROP and the fault-related information The scenario is implemented in two demonstrators. The ﬁrst demonstrator focuses on assisted operation. Therefore, the connection between the machine and the ROP is established by the MX-Port conﬁguration Orion, whereas the discovery process and the ﬁnding of the data source are modeled using AAS. The data source itself is modeled with OPC UA. The Solution Provider can monitor the machine data as well as video streams from the machine and handle problematic situations remotely. The second demonstrator shows an au- tonomous fault handling scenario. The MX-Port conﬁguration Hercules is used in the use case. The required interfaces are described with the Asset Interfaces Description SM and the fault-related information are provided by the FaultDescription, SymptomDescription and FaultCorrection SMs. A process is described of how the autonomous fault handling is executed based on the provided SM. Furthermore, it is shown how further analysis can be executed via the ROP when it is necessary. Future work on the path to autonomy can tackle automatiza- tion topics outside the machinery itself to cover the complete production process from the order to delivery. Therefore, a more detailed level of semantics and artiﬁcial intelligence can be integrated. Additionally, the whole process can be more ﬂexible and automatized using agentic frameworks.

# Page 8^page=8
REFERENCES [1] M. Hermann, P. R¨ubel, M. Birtel, F. Mohr, A. Wagner, and M. Ruskowski, “Self-description of cyber-physical production modules for a product driven manufacturing system,” Procedia Manufacturing, vol. 38, pp. 291–298, 2019. [3] German Chamber of Commerce and Industry (DIHK), “Skilled labour report 2025/2026: Challenges persist in manufacturing and engineering,” February 2026. [Online].
